message.content:
Design an implementation plan to make the @belt/ai package fully tree-shakeable.

## Current State (from exploration):

**vite.config.ts:**
- Single entry point: src/index.ts
- Outputs: ES (.mjs) + CJS (.cjs)
- Uses: vite-plugin-dts with rollupTypes: true

**package.json (minimal):**
```json
{
  "name": "@belt/ai",
  "description": "Belt utilities to work with AI",
  "scripts": { "build": "vite build" },
  "dependencies": { "@belt/tools": "0.0.1" }
}
```

**Source structure:**
```
src/
â”śâ”€â”€ index.ts    (barrel: export { createHook, createHooks } from "./hooks")
â””â”€â”€ hooks.ts    (actual implementation)
```

## Problems Identified:
1. No "exports" field in package.json
2. No "sideEffects": false declaration
3. No "main", "module", "types" fields
4. Single entry point prevents granular imports
5. vite-plugin-dts with rollupTypes: true bundles all types into one file

## Requirements:
1. Enable tree-shaking for consumers using Webpack, Rollup, esbuild, Vite
2. Allow granular imports: `import { createHook } from '@belt/ai/hooks'`
3. Maintain backwards compatibility: `import { createHook } from '@belt/ai'`
4. Generate separate .d.ts files per entry point
5. Mark package as side-effect free

## Design a complete plan covering:
1. package.json changes (exports, sideEffects, main, module, types)
2. vite.config.ts changes (multiple entry points, dts config)
3. Any source file restructuring if needed
4. Build output structure

Working directory: /Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/mms-last-attempt/packages/belt-ai

content.content:
Web search results for query: "vite library mode tree shaking multiple entry points exports 2025"

Links: [{"title":"How to build a tree-shakable library with Vite and Rollup - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/morewings/how-to-build-a-tree-shakable-library-with-vite-and-rollup-16cb"},{"title":"vite-plugin-entry-shaking - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/vite-plugin-entry-shaking"},{"title":"Library mode output not tree-shakeable when consumed by webpack Â· Issue #5174 Â· vitejs/vite","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/issues/5174"},{"title":"How can I tree-shake unused imports in a production build? Â· vitejs/vite Â· Discussion #13171","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/discussions/13171"},{"title":"GitHub - kouts/vite-treeshakable-library: A Vite template to create a treeshakable library","url":"https://github.com/kouts/vite-treeshakable-library"},{"title":"Create a Component Library Fastđźš€(using Vite's library mode) - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/receter/how-to-create-a-react-component-library-using-vites-library-mode-4lma"},{"title":"Tree-Shaking CSS in a Vite project â€“ Syed Umar Anis","url":"https://umaranis.com/2025/02/01/tree-shaking-css-in-a-vite-project/"},{"title":"GitHub - Dschungelabenteuer/vite-plugin-entry-shaking: Mimic tree-shaking behaviour when importing code from an entry file in development mode.","url":"https://github.com/Dschungelabenteuer/vite-plugin-entry-shaking"},{"title":"Tree shaking dynamic import from VIte bundled React component library Â· Issue #17460 Â· vitejs/vite","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/issues/17460"},{"title":"GitHub - georgwittberger/vite-plugin-entrypoints: Vite plugin to generate additional entry points for files matching glob patterns","url":"https://github.com/georgwittberger/vite-plugin-entrypoints"}]

Here are the search results for "vite library mode tree shaking multiple entry points exports 2025":

## Key Findings

### The Core Problem
A significant drawback of Rollup's standard ES Module bundling strategy is its negative impact on tree shaking in bundlers like Webpack and Next.js. By compiling all module code into a single ./dist/index.js file, even a small import can result in the entire library and its dependencies being included in your application.(no content)When writing a library using vite "library mode," the output is expected to be tree-shakeable regardless of where it is consumed. When consumed by vite, things work properly, but when consumed by webpack, the output is not tree-shakeable.

### Solution 1: Use `preserveModules`
Fortunately, Rollup offers a solution to this tree shaking challenge. By enabling the preserveModules setting, we can maintain the original module structure, significantly improving tree shaking capabilities.

### Solution 2: Multiple Entry Points
In vite.config.ts, the build.lib.entry option defines the entry points for the library. The createEntries function dynamically generates an entry point for each file in the lib/ directory (such as .ts and .vue files), allowing Rollup to split them into separate chunks. This approach ensures effective tree-shaking by including only the used code in the final bundle.(no content)The recommended wayâ€”instead of using output.preserveModules that may tree-shake exports as well as emit virtual files created by pluginsâ€”is to turn every file into an entry point.(no content)In order to create a tree-shakable output we have to apply one of the code splitting techniques supported by rollup.js. One common practice is to define multiple entry points to force rollup.js to split code into chunks.

### Solution 3: Subpath Exports in package.json
Using subpath exports in package.json like `"./featureA": "./src/featureA.ts"` allows consumers to import only what's needed: `import { featureA } from "my-library/featureA"`. Now, only the necessary styles are imported in both development and production.

### Development Mode Considerations
In development mode, when Vite serves a module, the browser loads the shared/index.ts file which initiates requests for all of the modules it relies on. As your project and entry points grow, you end up paying the price for the ever-increasing amount of unnecessary requests and HMR updates, which consumes time and resources.

There's a plugin called **vite-plugin-entry-shaking** that helps with this: it mimics tree-shaking behaviour when importing code from an entry file in development mode.

### Important Package.json Configuration
Setting `"sideEffects": false` in package.json is important for proper tree-shaking.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: Extract the key configuration for vite.config.ts to make a library tree-shakeable, including: 1) build.lib.entry configuration for multiple entry points, 2) rollupOptions.output.preserveModules settings, 3) package.json exports field structure, 4) Any other critical settings for tree-shaking

Extract the complete vite.config.ts configuration and package.json exports field configuration for creating a tree-shakeable library. Focus on: entry points, preserveModules, sideEffects, exports field structure

content.content:
# Tree-Shakeable Library Configuration

Based on the documentation, here's the key configuration approach:

## Vite Configuration Strategy

The template uses a **dynamic entry points approach** rather than `preserveModules`. The `createEntries` function generates individual entry points for each file in the `lib/` directory (`.ts` and `.vue` files), allowing Rollup to split them into separate chunks.

Key insight: "This approach ensures effective tree-shaking by including only the used code in the final bundle. It also excludes unnecessary files, such as tests or Storybook stories."

## Entry Points Pattern

The configuration creates entries like:
```
lib/components/index.ts
lib/utils/index.ts
lib/assets/lib.css
```

Each gets its own chunk, enabling granular consumption.

## Package.json Exports Structure

The library exports CSS alongside component code:
```
@kouts/vite-treeshakable-library/assets/lib.css
```

This allows consumers to import theme tokens and custom utilities independently.

## SideEffects Declaration

While not explicitly detailed in the excerpt, the tree-shaking strategy relies on proper `sideEffects: false` configuration to mark the library as side-effect-free.

## Path Aliases

Aliases resolve to relative paths during build via `resolve-tspaths`, ensuring bundled output works without path resolution dependencies.

This design prioritizes **granular entry points over monolithic exports**, maximizing tree-shaking potential for consumers.

# Tree-Shakeable Library Configuration

Based on the documentation, here's the key configuration approach:

## Vite Configuration Strategy

The template uses a **dynamic entry points approach** rather than `preserveModules`. The `createEntries` function generates individual entry points for each file in the `lib/` directory (`.ts` and `.vue` files), allowing Rollup to split them into separate chunks.

Key insight: "This approach ensures effective tree-shaking by including only the used code in the final bundle. It also excludes unnecessary files, such as tests or Storybook stories."

## Entry Points Pattern

The configuration creates entries like:
```
lib/components/index.ts
lib/utils/index.ts
lib/assets/lib.css
```

Each gets its own chunk, enabling granular consumption.

## Package.json Exports Structure

The library exports CSS alongside component code:
```
@kouts/vite-treeshakable-library/assets/lib.css
```

This allows consumers to import theme tokens and custom utilities independently.

## SideEffects Declaration

While not explicitly detailed in the excerpt, the tree-shaking strategy relies on proper `sideEffects: false` configuration to mark the library as side-effect-free.

## Path Aliases

Aliases resolve to relative paths during build via `resolve-tspaths`, ensuring bundled output works without path resolution dependencies.

This design prioritizes **granular entry points over monolithic exports**, maximizing tree-shaking potential for consumers.

Â· vitejs/vite Â· Discussion #1736","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/discussions/1736"},{"title":"Create a Component Library Fastđźš€(using Vite's library mode) - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/receter/how-to-create-a-react-component-library-using-vites-library-mode-4lma"},{"title":"vite-plugin-dts - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/vite-plugin-dts"},{"title":"multiple entry got one .d.ts to one entry, how to clean them left index only Â· Issue #262 Â· qmhc/vite-plugin-dts","url":"https://github.com/qmhc/vite-plugin-dts/issues/262"},{"title":"qmhc/vite-plugin-dts: An unplugin for generating ...","url":"https://github.com/qmhc/unplugin-dts"},{"title":"Generating Declaration Files - Vite | Frontend Masters","url":"https://frontendmasters.com/courses/vite/generating-declaration-files/"},{"title":"Generate different declaration types Â· Issue #267 Â· qmhc/unplugin-dts","url":"https://github.com/qmhc/unplugin-dts/issues/267"},{"title":"Configure Vite on your Nx workspace | Nx","url":"https://nx.dev/docs/technologies/build-tools/vite/guides/configure-vite"},{"title":"Plugin API | Vite","url":"https://vite.dev/guide/api-plugin"},{"title":"Folder as entry in library mode for multiple independent js modules Â· vitejs/vite Â· Discussion #8098","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/discussions/8098"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about `vite-plugin-dts`, `preserveModules`, and generating separate `.d.ts` files for multiple entry points:

## Key Findings

### Default Behavior
`vite-plugin-dts` is a Vite plugin that generates declaration files (`*.d.ts`) from `.ts(x)` or `.vue` source files when using Vite in library mode. By default, the generated declaration files are following the source structure.

### Recommended Approach for Multiple Entry Points
If you want to convert a set of files to another format while maintaining the file structure and export signatures, the recommended wayâ€”instead of using `output.preserveModules` that may tree-shake exports as well as emit virtual files created by pluginsâ€”is to turn every file into an entry point.

### Using `preserveModules` with Multiple Entries
You can configure `preserveModules: true` in rollupOptions output, combined with using `glob.sync` to specify multiple entry points.

### Configuration Example
A common pattern uses `fast-glob` to define an array of entry points like `['src/common/**/*.ts', 'src/utils.ts', 'src/index.ts']`, searches for files matching those patterns, and maps the file paths to key-value pairs.

### Merging vs. Separate Files
If you want to merge all declarations into one file instead of separate files, just specify `rollupTypes: true`.

### Multi-Entry Issues with `rollupTypes`
When using `rollupTypes: true` with multiple entries, there can be issues where it reports errors like "The mainEntryPointFilePath path does not exist." Using `insertTypesEntry: true` instead works better for multi-entry scenarios.

### unplugin-dts (Evolved from vite-plugin-dts)
There's also `unplugin-dts`, an unplugin that generates declaration files (`*.d.ts`) from `.ts(x)` or `.vue` source files when using library mode. It evolved from `vite-plugin-dts`, and after being rewritten with unplugin, it has become more versatile.

### Configuring dts Include
Per default, dts will generate types for both src and lib because both folders are included in the project's .tsconfig. You can pass a configuration parameter like `include: ['lib']` to control which directories are processed.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "package.json exports field subpath exports conditional exports types import require 2025"

Links: [{"title":"Modules: Packages | Node.js v25.2.1 Documentation","url":"https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html"},{"title":"Guide to the package.json `exports` field | Hiroki Osame","url":"https://hirok.io/posts/package-json-exports"},{"title":"Package exports | webpack","url":"https://webpack.js.org/guides/package-exports/"},{"title":"TypeScript: Documentation - Modules - Reference","url":"https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/modules/reference.html"},{"title":"Package Exports Support (New) | Metro","url":"https://metrobundler.dev/docs/package-exports/"},{"title":"packages","url":"https://www.cs.unb.ca/~bremner/teaching/cs2613/books/nodejs-api/packages/"},{"title":"Modern Package.json Exports - A Complete Guide | JuanmanTech - its Me Juanman!","url":"https://juanman.tech/blog/9"},{"title":"Modules: ECMAScript modules | Node.js v25.2.1 Documentation","url":"https://nodejs.org/api/esm.html"},{"title":"Types that are found, but not exported in package.json, should still be usable Â· Issue #52363 Â· microsoft/TypeScript","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/52363"},{"title":"Dependency resolution","url":"https://parceljs.org/features/dependency-resolution/"}]

Here's a summary of what I found about `package.json` exports field, subpath exports, conditional exports, and TypeScript types:

## The `exports` Field in package.json

"The "exports" provides a modern alternative to "main" allowing multiple entry points to be defined, conditional entry resolution support between environments, and preventing any other entry points besides those defined in "exports"."

### Key Features

**Subpath Exports:**
To define multiple entry points, the exports field can be set to a subpaths object, where each key starts with a `.`. The `.` key indicates the main package entry, and subpaths start with `./`.(no content)While this is similar to the main field, there's a notable distinction: as soon as you use exports, it black-boxes your package. This means no subpaths (not even package.json) are accessible by default unless explicitly specified.

**Conditional Exports:**
Conditional exports can be used within "exports" to define different package entry points per environment, including whether the package is referenced via require or via import.(no content)exports also supports different targets for a given subpath dependent on conditions, such as whether building for a browser or Node.js, and whether import or require is used by the consumer.

### Condition Order and Priority

Within the "exports" object, key order is significant. During condition matching, earlier entries have higher priority and take precedence over later entries. The general rule is that conditions should be from most specific to least specific in object order.(no content)"default" - the generic fallback that always matches. Can be a CommonJS or ES module file. This condition should always come last.

### TypeScript Integration

When resolving through conditional "exports", TypeScript always matches the "types" and "default" conditions if present. Additionally, TypeScript will match a versioned types condition in the form "types@{selector}" according to the same version-matching rules implemented in "typesVersions".(no content)For each module specifier being resolved, the syntax and the module format of the importing file are first used to determine whether the module specifier will be in an import or require in the emitted JavaScript. That information is then passed into the module resolver to determine which resolution algorithm to use (and whether to use the "import" or "require" condition for package.json "exports" or "imports").

### Example with Types, Import, and Require

From the search results, here's a modern pattern for TypeScript packages:

```json
{
  "name": "my-package",
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
      "import": "./dist/index.mjs",
      "require": "./dist/index.cjs"
    },
    "./utils": {
      "types": "./dist/utils/index.d.ts",
      "import": "./dist/utils/index.mjs",
      "require": "./dist/utils/index.cjs"
    }
  }
}
```

More specific paths are preferred over less specific ones. The exports field is preferred over other package entry fields like main, module, browser or custom ones.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Here's a summary of what I found about `package.json` exports field, subpath exports, conditional exports, and TypeScript types:

## The `exports` Field in package.json

"The "exports" provides a modern alternative to "main" allowing multiple entry points to be defined, conditional entry resolution support between environments, and preventing any other entry points besides those defined in "exports"."

### Key Features

**Subpath Exports:**
To define multiple entry points, the exports field can be set to a subpaths object, where each key starts with a `.`. The `.` key indicates the main package entry, and subpaths start with `./`.(no content)While this is similar to the main field, there's a notable distinction: as soon as you use exports, it black-boxes your package. This means no subpaths (not even package.json) are accessible by default unless explicitly specified.

**Conditional Exports:**
Conditional exports can be used within "exports" to define different package entry points per environment, including whether the package is referenced via require or via import.(no content)exports also supports different targets for a given subpath dependent on conditions, such as whether building for a browser or Node.js, and whether import or require is used by the consumer.

### Condition Order and Priority

Within the "exports" object, key order is significant. During condition matching, earlier entries have higher priority and take precedence over later entries. The general rule is that conditions should be from most specific to least specific in object order.(no content)"default" - the generic fallback that always matches. Can be a CommonJS or ES module file. This condition should always come last.

### TypeScript Integration

When resolving through conditional "exports", TypeScript always matches the "types" and "default" conditions if present. Additionally, TypeScript will match a versioned types condition in the form "types@{selector}" according to the same version-matching rules implemented in "typesVersions".(no content)For each module specifier being resolved, the syntax and the module format of the importing file are first used to determine whether the module specifier will be in an import or require in the emitted JavaScript. That information is then passed into the module resolver to determine which resolution algorithm to use (and whether to use the "import" or "require" condition for package.json "exports" or "imports").

### Example with Types, Import, and Require

From the search results, here's a modern pattern for TypeScript packages:

```json
{
  "name": "my-package",
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
      "import": "./dist/index.mjs",
      "require": "./dist/index.cjs"
    },
    "./utils": {
      "types": "./dist/utils/index.d.ts",
      "import": "./dist/utils/index.mjs",
      "require": "./dist/utils/index.cjs"
    }
  }
}
```

More specific paths are preferred over less specific ones. The exports field is preferred over other package entry fields like main, module, browser or custom ones.

input.prompt: Extract the complete guide for package.json exports field, specifically: 1) How to structure exports for subpaths, 2) Conditional exports with types/import/require, 3) Wildcard patterns, 4) Best practices for TypeScript libraries

content.content:
# Complete Guide: package.json `exports` Field

## 1) Structuring Exports for Subpaths

The `exports` field uses a subpaths object where each key starts with a dot. The `.` key represents the main entry point, while `./` prefixes define additional subpaths.

**Basic structure:**
```json
{
  "exports": {
    ".": "./dist/index.js",
    "./utils": "./dist/utils.js",
    "./helpers": "./dist/dir/helpers.js"
  }
}
```

Key principles:
- Use exact subpaths (e.g., `./utils`) for straightforward mapping to specific files
- Pattern matching with `*` captures nested paths: `"./*": "./dist/*"` exposes everything in the dist directory
- Block sensitive paths using `null`: `"./dist/internal/*": null` prevents access to internal modules
- All subpath keys must begin with `.`

**Protecting internal files:** Once you define `exports`, your package becomes "black-boxed"â€”only explicitly listed subpaths are accessible. This establishes a clear public API, preventing unintended imports of internal files and giving maintainers flexibility during restructuring.

## 2) Conditional Exports with Types, Import, and Require

Conditional exports resolve different files based on how the package is imported and what environment is running the code.

**Multi-format support:**
```json
{
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "require": {
        "types": "./dist/index.d.cts",
        "default": "./dist/index.cjs"
      },
      "import": {
        "types": "./dist/index.d.mts",
        "default": "./dist/index.mjs"
      }
    },
    "./feature": {
      "require": {
        "types": "./dist/feature.d.cts",
        "default": "./dist/feature.cjs"
      },
      "import": {
        "types": "./dist/feature.d.mts",
        "default": "./dist/feature.mjs"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Critical TypeScript considerations:**
- Use separate type files: `.d.cts` for CommonJS, `.d.mts` for ESM. TypeScript infers module format from file extensions and will misinterpret a shared `.d.ts` file
- Order matters: `types` must precede `default` within each condition block
- Consumers require `moduleResolution` set to `Node16`, `NodeNext`, or `Bundler` in `tsconfig.json`

**Condition ordering:** Conditions are prioritized by key order, with custom conditions placed before default ones (`require`, `import`, `default`). Anything below default conditions cannot be reached.

## 3) Wildcard Patterns

The `*` character in subpath patterns captures any string, including nested paths.

**Examples:**

- `"./*": "./*"` â€” Exposes all package files (use cautiously; even minor interface changes become breaking changes)
- `"./dist/*": "./dist/*"` â€” Limits exposure to the dist directory only
- `"./dist/internal/*": null` â€” Explicitly blocks specific directories while allowing others

**Resolution behavior:** In CommonJS, implicit file extensions are disabled with subpath patterns (requiring explicit `.js`). In ESM, patterns can omit extensions: `"./*": "./dist/*.js"`

## 4) Best Practices for TypeScript Libraries

**Comprehensive setup:**
1. **Separate entry points** with distinct type and code files for each module format
2. **Map subpaths cleanly** â€” leverage `exports` to avoid exposing internal `dist` directory paths in imports
3. **Validate your package** using tools like [publint](https://publint.dev) (checks format consistency) or [Are the Types Wrong?](https://arethetypeswrong.github.io) (detects TypeScript issues)
4. **Combine exports with subpath exports** to support multiple features while maintaining backward compatibility
5. **Restrict internal access** â€” don't expose wildcard patterns unless necessary; be specific about which directories consumers can access

**Ecosystem compatibility:** Modern tools including Vite, TypeScript, esbuild, Webpack, and Parcel all support `exports` with conditional resolution, making it a safe choice for library authors.

# Complete Guide: package.json `exports` Field

## 1) Structuring Exports for Subpaths

The `exports` field uses a subpaths object where each key starts with a dot. The `.` key represents the main entry point, while `./` prefixes define additional subpaths.

**Basic structure:**
```json
{
  "exports": {
    ".": "./dist/index.js",
    "./utils": "./dist/utils.js",
    "./helpers": "./dist/dir/helpers.js"
  }
}
```

Key principles:
- Use exact subpaths (e.g., `./utils`) for straightforward mapping to specific files
- Pattern matching with `*` captures nested paths: `"./*": "./dist/*"` exposes everything in the dist directory
- Block sensitive paths using `null`: `"./dist/internal/*": null` prevents access to internal modules
- All subpath keys must begin with `.`

**Protecting internal files:** Once you define `exports`, your package becomes "black-boxed"â€”only explicitly listed subpaths are accessible. This establishes a clear public API, preventing unintended imports of internal files and giving maintainers flexibility during restructuring.

## 2) Conditional Exports with Types, Import, and Require

Conditional exports resolve different files based on how the package is imported and what environment is running the code.

**Multi-format support:**
```json
{
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "require": {
        "types": "./dist/index.d.cts",
        "default": "./dist/index.cjs"
      },
      "import": {
        "types": "./dist/index.d.mts",
        "default": "./dist/index.mjs"
      }
    },
    "./feature": {
      "require": {
        "types": "./dist/feature.d.cts",
        "default": "./dist/feature.cjs"
      },
      "import": {
        "types": "./dist/feature.d.mts",
        "default": "./dist/feature.mjs"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Critical TypeScript considerations:**
- Use separate type files: `.d.cts` for CommonJS, `.d.mts` for ESM. TypeScript infers module format from file extensions and will misinterpret a shared `.d.ts` file
- Order matters: `types` must precede `default` within each condition block
- Consumers require `moduleResolution` set to `Node16`, `NodeNext`, or `Bundler` in `tsconfig.json`

**Condition ordering:** Conditions are prioritized by key order, with custom conditions placed before default ones (`require`, `import`, `default`). Anything below default conditions cannot be reached.

## 3) Wildcard Patterns

The `*` character in subpath patterns captures any string, including nested paths.

**Examples:**

- `"./*": "./*"` â€” Exposes all package files (use cautiously; even minor interface changes become breaking changes)
- `"./dist/*": "./dist/*"` â€” Limits exposure to the dist directory only
- `"./dist/internal/*": null` â€” Explicitly blocks specific directories while allowing others

**Resolution behavior:** In CommonJS, implicit file extensions are disabled with subpath patterns (requiring explicit `.js`). In ESM, patterns can omit extensions: `"./*": "./dist/*.js"`

## 4) Best Practices for TypeScript Libraries

**Comprehensive setup:**
1. **Separate entry points** with distinct type and code files for each module format
2. **Map subpaths cleanly** â€” leverage `exports` to avoid exposing internal `dist` directory paths in imports
3. **Validate your package** using tools like [publint](https://publint.dev) (checks format consistency) or [Are the Types Wrong?](https://arethetypeswrong.github.io) (detects TypeScript issues)
4. **Combine exports with subpath exports** to support multiple features while maintaining backward compatibility
5. **Restrict internal access** â€” don't expose wildcard patterns unless necessary; be specific about which directories consumers can access

**Ecosystem compatibility:** Modern tools including Vite, TypeScript, esbuild, Webpack, and Parcel all support `exports` with conditional resolution, making it a safe choice for library authors.

content.text: Now let me research the specific vite-plugin-dts configuration options by looking at its GitHub repository:

input.prompt: Extract the configuration options for vite-plugin-dts, specifically: 1) rollupTypes option and when to use false, 2) insertTypesEntry option, 3) How to generate separate .d.ts files per module, 4) Configuration for multiple entry points

content.content:
# unplugin-dts Configuration Options

## 1. rollupTypes (bundleTypes) Option

**When to use `false`:**
The documentation indicates bundling types requires `@microsoft/api-extractor`, which is described as a "time-intensive operation." Set to `false` (the default) when you prefer keeping separate `.d.ts` files matching your source structure without the overhead of bundling.

## 2. insertTypesEntry Option

This option controls whether declaration entry files are generated. When enabled, the plugin "uses package.json `types` property if it exists or `${outDir}/index.d.ts`." The default is `false`, but it's "forced to `true` when `rollupTypes` is `true`."

## 3. Generating Separate .d.ts Files Per Module

By default, the plugin maintains the source file structure in output. Leave `bundleTypes` disabled and avoid setting `insertTypesEntry: true` to preserve individual module declaration files. This approach generates files "following the source structure" without consolidation.

## 4. Multiple Entry Points Configuration

The options support flexible entry point handling through:
- **`outDir`**: Accepts an array for "output to multiple directories"
- **`entryRoot`**: Override the root path, useful for monorepos where "the output path of each file will be calculated based on the value provided"

Vite/Rollup configurations define multiple inputs via their native `entry` or `input` objects; unplugin-dts processes them accordingly.

toolUseResult.result:
# unplugin-dts Configuration Options

## 1. rollupTypes (bundleTypes) Option

**When to use `false`:**
The documentation indicates bundling types requires `@microsoft/api-extractor`, which is described as a "time-intensive operation." Set to `false` (the default) when you prefer keeping separate `.d.ts` files matching your source structure without the overhead of bundling.

## 2. insertTypesEntry Option

This option controls whether declaration entry files are generated. When enabled, the plugin "uses package.json `types` property if it exists or `${outDir}/index.d.ts`." The default is `false`, but it's "forced to `true` when `rollupTypes` is `true`."

## 3. Generating Separate .d.ts Files Per Module

By default, the plugin maintains the source file structure in output. Leave `bundleTypes` disabled and avoid setting `insertTypesEntry: true` to preserve individual module declaration files. This approach generates files "following the source structure" without consolidation.

## 4. Multiple Entry Points Configuration

The options support flexible entry point handling through:
- **`outDir`**: Accepts an array for "output to multiple directories"
- **`entryRoot`**: Override the root path, useful for monorepos where "the output path of each file will be calculated based on the value provided"

Vite/Rollup configurations define multiple inputs via their native `entry` or `input` objects; unplugin-dts processes them accordingly.

content.text:
Now I have all the information needed. Let me provide a comprehensive implementation plan.

---

## Plano de Implementacao: Tree-Shaking para @belt/ai

### Resumo do Problema

O pacote `@belt/ai` atualmente nao e tree-shakeable devido a:

1. **Ausencia do campo `exports`** no package.json - bundlers nao sabem quais subpaths estao disponiveis
2. **Ausencia de `sideEffects: false`** - bundlers assumem que todo codigo pode ter efeitos colaterais
3. **Ausencia de `main`, `module`, `types`** - campos legados necessarios para retrocompatibilidade
4. **Entry point unico** no vite.config.ts - gera um unico bundle ao inves de modulos separados
5. **`rollupTypes: true`** no vite-plugin-dts - bundleia todos os tipos em um arquivo unico

---

### Plano Detalhado

#### FASE 1: Alteracoes no package.json

**Campos a adicionar:**

```json
{
  "name": "@belt/ai",
  "version": "0.0.1",
  "description": "Belt utilities to work with AI",
  "type": "module",
  "sideEffects": false,
  "main": "./dist/index.cjs",
  "module": "./dist/index.mjs",
  "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
      "import": "./dist/index.mjs",
      "require": "./dist/index.cjs"
    },
    "./hooks": {
      "types": "./dist/hooks.d.ts",
      "import": "./dist/hooks.mjs",
      "require": "./dist/hooks.cjs"
    }
  },
  "files": [
    "dist"
  ],
  "scripts": {
    "build": "vite build"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@belt/tools": "0.0.1"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    ...
  }
}
```

**Justificativas:**

| Campo | Razao |
|-------|-------|
| `type: "module"` | Declara ESM como formato padrao |
| `sideEffects: false` | Permite tree-shaking - informa bundlers que modulos sem uso podem ser removidos com seguranca |
| `main` | Retrocompatibilidade com Node.js antigo e bundlers que nao suportam `exports` |
| `module` | Retrocompatibilidade com bundlers que preferem ESM via este campo |
| `types` | Retrocompatibilidade para TypeScript com `moduleResolution: "node"` |
| `exports` | Campo moderno que define subpaths e condicoes (types/import/require) |
| `files` | Controla quais arquivos sao publicados no npm |

**Sobre a estrutura de `exports`:**

- O campo `types` **deve vir antes** de `import`/`require` - ordem e significativa ([fonte](https://hirok.io/posts/package-json-exports))
- Cada subpath (`.`, `./hooks`) define seu proprio conjunto de condicoes
- Isto permite: `import { createHook } from '@belt/ai'` e `import { createHook } from '@belt/ai/hooks'`

---

#### FASE 2: Alteracoes no vite.config.ts

**Configuracao proposta:**

```typescript
import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react-swc';
import { resolve } from 'path';
import nodeExternals from 'rollup-plugin-node-externals';
import { defineConfig, UserConfig } from 'vite';
import dts from 'vite-plugin-dts';
import { externalizeDeps } from 'vite-plugin-externalize-deps';
import tsconfigPaths from 'vite-tsconfig-paths';

export default defineConfig(() => {
  const config: UserConfig = {
    build: {
      lib: {
        // MUDANCA: Multiplos entry points
        entry: {
          index: resolve(__dirname, 'src/index.ts'),
          hooks: resolve(__dirname, 'src/hooks.ts'),
        },
        name: 'belt-ai',
        formats: ['es', 'cjs'],
        fileName: (format, entryName) => {
          const ext = format === 'es' ? 'mjs' : 'cjs';
          return `${entryName}.${ext}`;
        },
      },
      rollupOptions: {
        external: ['react', 'react-dom'],
        output: {
          // IMPORTANTE: Nao usar preserveModules aqui
          // Multiplos entry points ja garantem tree-shaking
          // preserveModules pode causar problemas com plugins
        },
      },
      sourcemap: true,
      minify: 'terser',
    },
    plugins: [
      externalizeDeps(),
      dts({
        // MUDANCA CRITICA: Desabilitar rollupTypes
        rollupTypes: false,
        tsconfigPath: 'tsconfig.prod.json',
        // Gera .d.ts separados para cada entry point
      }),
      {
        enforce: 'pre',
        ...nodeExternals(),
      },
      react({}),
      tsconfigPaths(),
    ],
  };

  return config;
});
```

**Mudancas criticas:**

| Mudanca | Antes | Depois | Razao |
|---------|-------|--------|-------|
| `entry` | String unica | Objeto com multiplas entradas | Permite imports granulares |
| `fileName` | Template fixo | Funcao dinamica | Gera nomes corretos para cada entry |
| `rollupTypes` | `true` | `false` | Gera .d.ts separados por modulo |

**Por que NAO usar `preserveModules`:**

Segundo a [documentacao pesquisada](https://github.com/vitejs/vite/discussions/1736):
> "The recommended way -- instead of using output.preserveModules that may tree-shake exports as well as emit virtual files created by plugins -- is to turn every file into an entry point."

Multiplos entry points e a abordagem recomendada.

---

#### FASE 3: Estrutura de Build Resultante

Apos as mudancas, o diretorio `dist/` tera:

```
dist/
â”śâ”€â”€ index.mjs      # ESM barrel (re-exporta hooks)
â”śâ”€â”€ index.cjs      # CJS barrel
â”śâ”€â”€ index.d.ts     # Types barrel
â”śâ”€â”€ hooks.mjs      # ESM hooks direto
â”śâ”€â”€ hooks.cjs      # CJS hooks direto
â””â”€â”€ hooks.d.ts     # Types hooks direto
```

**Imports possiveis pelo consumidor:**

```typescript
// Via barrel (retrocompativel)
import { createHook, createHooks } from '@belt/ai';

// Via subpath (granular, otimo para tree-shaking)
import { createHook } from '@belt/ai/hooks';
```

---

#### FASE 4: Nenhuma Mudanca Necessaria nos Arquivos Fonte

A estrutura atual de `src/` ja esta adequada:

```
src/
â”śâ”€â”€ index.ts    # export { createHook, createHooks } from "./hooks"
â””â”€â”€ hooks.ts    # implementacao real
```

O `index.ts` funciona como barrel que re-exporta de `hooks.ts`. Com multiplos entry points:
- Consumidores que importam de `@belt/ai` usam o barrel
- Consumidores que importam de `@belt/ai/hooks` bypassa o barrel

---

### Validacao do Plano

**Checklist de tree-shaking:**

- [x] `sideEffects: false` declarado
- [x] `exports` com subpaths condicionais
- [x] Multiplos entry points no Vite
- [x] .d.ts separados (rollupTypes: false)
- [x] Campos legados para retrocompatibilidade (main, module, types)
- [x] Formatos ESM e CJS suportados

**Compatibilidade com bundlers:**

| Bundler | Suporte |
|---------|---------|
| Webpack 5+ | Sim (usa `exports` + `sideEffects`) |
| Rollup | Sim (usa `exports`) |
| esbuild | Sim (usa `exports`) |
| Vite | Sim (usa `exports`) |
| Parcel 2+ | Sim (usa `exports`) |

---

### Riscos e Mitigacoes

| Risco | Probabilidade | Mitigacao |
|-------|---------------|-----------|
| TypeScript nao resolve types em subpaths | Media | Testar com `moduleResolution: "bundler"` e `"node16"` |
| Consumidores com bundlers antigos | Baixa | Campos `main`/`module`/`types` garantem fallback |
| .d.ts nao gerados corretamente | Media | Verificar saida do build manualmente |

---

### Sequencia de Implementacao

1. **Atualizar package.json** - adicionar todos os campos
2. **Atualizar vite.config.ts** - multiplos entries + rollupTypes: false
3. **Executar build** - `bun run build`
4. **Verificar dist/** - confirmar estrutura de arquivos
5. **Testar imports** - criar projeto consumidor de teste
6. **Validar com publint** - ferramenta que valida package.json ([publint.dev](https://publint.dev))

---

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/mms-last-attempt/packages/belt-ai/package.json` - Adicionar exports, sideEffects, main, module, types
- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/mms-last-attempt/packages/belt-ai/vite.config.ts` - Configurar multiplos entry points e rollupTypes: false
- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/mms-last-attempt/packages/belt-ai/src/index.ts` - Barrel existente (nao precisa mudar, apenas referencia)
- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/mms-last-attempt/packages/belt-ai/src/hooks.ts` - Implementacao existente (nao precisa mudar, apenas referencia)
- `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/mms-last-attempt/packages/belt-ai/tsconfig.prod.json` - Verificar que declarationDir aponta para dist

---

### Fontes

- [How to build a tree-shakable library with Vite and Rollup](https://dev.to/morewings/how-to-build-a-tree-shakable-library-with-vite-and-rollup-16cb)
